topic.title: Bayes' Rule
topic.question: How does Bayes' rule update beliefs given evidence, and why is the posterior proportional to likelihood times prior?

promptVariant.name: Whiteboard prompt v1 - visual balance + pacing fixes

lesson.title: Bayes' Rule: Updating Beliefs with Evidence
lesson.payoff: Bayes' rule lets you rationally revise any belief the moment new data arrives -- powering spam filters, medical tests, and AI that improves with experience, all from one formula
staircase: probability as a degree of belief
why posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior
normalizing to get a true probability
lesson.destination: The learner holds the formula P(H|E) = P(E|H) * P(H) / P(E), knows that P(H) is the prior belief before evidence, P(E|H) is the likelihood of seeing E if H were true, P(E) is a normalizing constant, and understands that the posterior P(H|E) is proportional to P(E|H) * P(H) because P(E) is just a fixed scaling factor

narration.fullText: Suppose you wake up feeling sick. Before any test, you already have some belief about whether you have the flu -- call that your prior, written P of H, where H stands for the hypothesis that you have flu. The prior is your degree of belief before seeing any evidence. Now a doctor gives you a test and it comes back positive. How should that change your belief? Bayes' rule answers exactly this. Let's build it from first principles using a probability tree. A probability tree breaks every joint outcome into a chain of conditional steps. We write P of H and E -- the probability that both H and E are true -- in two equivalent ways. First, it equals P of E given H times P of H: the chance the test would be positive if you truly have flu, multiplied by your prior chance of having flu. Second, by symmetry, it also equals P of H given E times P of E. Setting these equal gives us Bayes' rule: P of H given E equals P of E given H times P of H, all divided by P of E. Let's name each piece. P of H given E is the posterior -- your updated belief after seeing the evidence. P of E given H is the likelihood -- how probable is this evidence if H were true? P of H is the prior -- your belief before any data. P of E is the marginal probability of the evidence -- a normalizing constant that makes the posterior sum to one across all hypotheses. Here is the key insight: P of E is just a fixed number for any particular piece of evidence. So the posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior. That proportionality symbol means we can compare hypotheses just by multiplying likelihood by prior, without even computing the full denominator. To turn proportionality into equality -- to get a true probability -- we divide by P of E, which is computed by summing likelihood times prior over every possible hypothesis. Our tree makes this concrete. Say P of H, the prior for flu, is 0.1 -- flu is uncommon. Say P of positive test given flu is 0.9 -- the test is usually right when you do have flu. The likelihood times prior is 0.9 times 0.1, which is 0.09. We also need the same product for the alternative: no flu. P of positive test given no flu is 0.05, and P of no flu is 0.9, giving 0.05 times 0.9 equals 0.045. P of E -- the denominator -- is 0.09 plus 0.045, which is 0.135. Dividing 0.09 by 0.135 gives a posterior of about 0.67. A positive test tripled your belief from 10 percent to 67 percent. That is Bayes' rule in action: evidence multiplies your prior belief, and a constant rescales the result into a valid probability.

beats.text: Now a doctor gives you a test and it comes back positive. How should that change your belief? Bayes' rule answers exactly this. Let's build it from first principles using a probability tree. A probability tree breaks every joint outcome into a chain of conditional steps.

We write P of H and E -- the probability that both H and E are true -- in two equivalent ways. First, it equals P of E given H times P of H. Second, by symmetry, it also equals P of H given E times P of E. Setting these equal gives us Bayes' rule: P of H given E equals P of E given H times P of H, all divided by P of E.

Let's name each piece. P of H given E is the posterior -- your updated belief after seeing the evidence. P of E given H is the likelihood -- how probable is this evidence if H were true? P of H is the prior -- your belief before any data. P of E is the marginal probability of the evidence -- a normalizing constant that makes the posterior sum to one across all hypotheses.

Here is the key insight: P of E is just a fixed number for any particular piece of evidence. So the posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior. That proportionality symbol means we can compare hypotheses just by multiplying likelihood by prior, without computing the full denominator. To turn proportionality into equality, we divide by P of E, computed by summing likelihood times prior over every hypothesis.
beats.whyThisBeat: why posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior

beats.text: Our tree makes this concrete. Say P of H, the prior for flu, is 0.1 -- flu is uncommon. The likelihood P of positive test given flu is 0.9. Likelihood times prior is 0.9 times 0.1, which is 0.09. For the alternative -- no flu -- likelihood times prior is 0.05 times 0.9, which is 0.045. The denominator P of E is 0.09 plus 0.045, equaling 0.135.

actions.content: Prior: P(H) = degree of belief before evidence

A probability tree rooted at a single node labeled 'Start' on the left. Two branches split right: upper branch labeled 'H (flu)  P(H)=0.1' leading to node H, lower branch labeled 'not H (no flu)  P(not H)=0.9' leading to node not-H. From node H, two sub-branches: upper labeled 'E+ given H  0.9' reaching leaf labeled 'H and E+', lower labeled 'E- given H  0.1' reaching leaf labeled 'H and E-'. From node not-H, two sub-branches: upper labeled 'E+ given not-H  0.05' reaching leaf labeled 'not-H and E+', lower labeled 'E- given not-H  0.95' reaching leaf labeled 'not-H and E-'. Leaves stacked on the right half.
box the numerator P(E|H) * P(H) in blue to distinguish it from the denominator
P(E|H) = likelihood  (how well H explains E)
underline the phrase 'proportional-to' in red to emphasize the core relationship

A horizontal probability bar spanning the full width of a small panel, divided into two segments: left segment spanning 10 percent of width labeled 'P(H)=0.1  flu' filled light blue, right segment spanning 90 percent labeled 'P(not H)=0.9  no flu' filled light grey. A vertical tick at the 10 percent mark. Panel labeled 'Prior beliefs' above it.
P(E) = sum over all H of [ P(E|H) * P(H) ]
circle the value 0.135 in green to mark it as the denominator going into the formula

A horizontal probability bar identical in width to bar_prior, now divided to show updated beliefs: left segment spanning 67 percent labeled 'P(H|E) = 0.67  flu' filled darker blue, right segment spanning 33 percent labeled 'P(not H|E) = 0.33  no flu' filled light grey. Panel labeled 'Posterior beliefs after positive test' above it, placed directly below bar_prior.
box the result 0.67 in red and draw a large upward arrow next to it annotated '+57% from prior'
underline the entire equation P(H|E) = [P(E|H)*P(H)] / P(E) in green as the final takeaway formula
